speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Moses Kaufman-- this is tape four. Moses, you were talking about-- your wife, Ala was working in the factory with you as well. What happened next? You were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the-- it was a horrible time for us in the ghetto, because already-- and I was always complaining to my-- Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]-- that it's impossible to live in such bad-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Conditions-- that I will be able-- not be able to fulfill my work. I was saying this in German, actually. I always-- when I have a word with him-- what has to be done, what was done, and this all-- so I told him, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have been many, many times telling him about the conditions in which we are living and that I would not be able to really fulfill my work. From the other side, I was frightened also, because working, you know, for the frightening, that-- that for-- for saboteurs, as-- that something could happen, you know? And I will not be able really to fill out the work for them. So he was [NON-ENGLISH]. Really, many, many times he was quite an [NON-ENGLISH]. They are doing every possible thing to take us out from the ghetto. Who-- who do you mean by us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, what they were working for the tanneries-- the Jews. And the Jews was waiting for the [NON-ENGLISH] no-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And that happened that it's quite possible the Gauleiter was Murer at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Gauleiter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gauleiter-- that is the head of the-- of Lithuania, of like a governor-- a governor-- a SR man, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Murer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-R-E-R. And they were most probably-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --giving him a lot of presents-- [LAUGHS] --maybe. And he gave the OK of taking out the [NON-ENGLISH] from the ghetto. And that was built. That was in-- in-- sorry-- in December '41. And that was built. Kailis, they called this, the building-- two blocks of flats. And 1,000-- about 1,500 people went out, because the-- the workers with the wives and the-- and the families out from ghetto and separate. They established two blocks on the-- on the name, Kailis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-I-L-I-E-I-S-- Kailis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far away from ghetto one was--

speaker: interviewee
text: From ghetto one, that was quite-- that was on the periphery-- how to call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Periphery.

speaker: interviewee
text: The periphery of the town, Vilna--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not far from the factory where the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] had been working, a little bit further from the tanneries. And we were also going in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's mean in a group-- in groups-- to work, and from work, coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: --oh, it was-- it was a lot better, because every family got a room for themself. There were three room or four room-- three, I believe-- a room-- flats, you know, with the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --entrance-- a big entrance, a bathroom, a kitchen, and so on. So all of this in every flat. That was three families living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were two blocks-- block number one, and block number two there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what apartment number you were in, or what room you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in block number one. And I had the room that was on the second floor-- on the second floor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the first room against the entrance of that flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the-- the two blocks where you lived, were they at all surrounded by guards, or walls, or fences?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, that was only by police in-- Jewish police what was formed. But that took only nearly a year that the SS made from that arbeiter block, [NON-ENGLISH] They make concentrations arbeiter block number one and number two. And, of course, that was a way to-- what they are calling the guards on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Towers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on towers, yes, on the four-- on the four corners of that block. And they were-- and they went by the entrance, a guard, a SS man that the times were the worst, that time. But still, we got the contact with the outside world by going to work and-- with working with Poles and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you describe that period of time that you were living in Kailis and going to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning was better, because we were more freed. We could bringing-- buying-- [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Provisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Provisions. And it was better than in ghettos. There is no comparison with the-- with the-- the conditions that are living in ghetto. It was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the SS took over, it start to get worst, of course. And selections starts to be-- the action for children till 13 years. Because people were still with the families there with children. The first bad action-- selection-- not selection-- it's for children till 13 years. So the all children were taken away-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and gassed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 194-- '42-- yes. I can't recollect time-- the time exactly when they--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was at Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kailis, yes. That was the worst action that I can describe-- children-- beautiful children, lovely children-- till 13 years-- till 13 years age. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] For us, what left over for the parents, for the mothers, for the fathers, it's not describable! [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the SS took control? You said there were actions and selections. Why did they choose--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --people who were working? Did the-- in the selections that followed from the camp of Kailis, were people who were selected taken out? Were they people who were working at the tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't follow you that properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any selections made by the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to take people out of Kailis, were any of the people who were selected people who were working also at the tannery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at the [NON-ENGLISH], in the-- in the-- in the tanneries--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --amongst the tanners, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend at Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till I escaped to the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --the other 1/2 '43-- 1943. While you were working-- living in Kailis and working at the tannery-- you said that conditions were better than at the ghetto. Were you able, as a result of having better conditions, were you able to help anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I helped, financially, even when I was in ghetto, the time which I was in ghetto, till establishing the Kailis block. I was helping people there. There are two families. And I got the written statements, a written-- a lot of material what was written about me, what I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you help them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Financially-- financially with money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money from to help them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- get? I will tell you. The-- during the Lithuanians, which taking over the high ranking positions that combine the Lithuanians, were stealing a lot of goods and selling them at the market. I don't know what they were doing later on. And to shut my mouth, they were throwing to me dollars-- not to open my mouth and not to see nothing. Amongst them, there was one on the name, Pikoris, Pikoris-- P-I-K-O-R-I-S. The other one was Butisas-- B-U-T-I-S-A-S. The others, I don't remember all the number-- the-- the names. Especially that Pikoris, always, when he was bribing me, so to say, he was throwing in me packs of dollars. And the money I used to help other Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the money into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding it-- not in the ghetto. I was hiding the money actually in the factories. And one might be there still existing. It would tell a lot of stories-- that. That what I am telling you is only just a-- just a drop. And whenever I need some money to help or to help for myself, I was getting help from that hiding, where the money was lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it lying?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was amongst-- that was in one of the big drier rooms there. And some of the bricks were taken out. That was a little-- where I was hiding that-- everything there. Being in ghetto, I was helping. You asked me who-- if I helped. That was one-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --Swirski. Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-W-I-R-S-K-E I-- sorry-- I-- for Swirski. And the story, I would do this in short. There were three brothers, three of four brothers. I can't tell you exactly. I believe there are three-- there are the three brothers. And they immigrated to Israel. And he was the one that a few months before the-- the war broke out between Russia and Germany, he came to Poland to liquidate some of sawmills-- to liquidate, to sell it, and-- and the war broke out. And he went out-- he, his wife, and his son-- and a one son. Once I was in the ghetto and just going with that [NON-ENGLISH]-- with another chap with the name Edelman. And he mentioned to me, showing me on a person, that he's sitting in-- next to a entrance in one of the houses there, and selling some timber, when you are starting a fire. I don't know how you call.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kindling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kindling. Yes. So I said to him, if you would know who that chap is and what he's doing now, you will-- if-- if you could help him, please, Kaufman, help him. So he went to him. He said, listen. That gentleman want to help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him, what kind he-- he wants to help. He wants to give you some money to help you. So he was-- no, no. No. He's only-- he's only giving here as a loan. Don't-- don't-- don't-- don't worry. Don't worry. Don't worry. So he accept this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got the written things about this, that I took out $500 and I give him $500. The other one was Alperowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-P-E-R-O-W-I-T-Z-- most probably. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- this is a family or just one person?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was he and his wife. That was in the ghetto, when I-- so long as I was in ghetto before I went to Kailis. And the same thing, one of my friends asked me if that is possible that he knows him and so on. He was from Kaunas, from Kovno, from Lithuania-- that he was a manufacturer, a owner of a-- a manufacturer from webbing things and so on. And he's in such a bad-- that he can't go to Kaunas. He not-- he is in Vilna. He's now in the ghetto and has to-- if it's possible if I could help him. I helped him too. And I got those from him. Letters are written about the help I gave him. That was the time when I was in ghetto. And Kailis-- I helped a lot of people too. And amongst others, that is the main help what I gave is to the director of the Yeshiva, of the Vilna Yeshiva, Rabbi Lvowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with his family and a few people from the Yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Kailis already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the rabbi's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lvowicz-- L-V-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: From time to time, I was helping him out always with big amounts of money-- thousands of dollars. And I got-- actually, I left the whole original papers I wrote. I got, because I hide them by one of the Poles, one of my friends-- Poles in-- in Vilna. And after the war, I got this out-- everything out. And he-- really, I helped him a tremendous lot-- a tremendous lot. He was arrived in Kailis different time-- different-- whenever-- when I was giving him a sum of money, 1,000 of the-- 2,000, or something like that. So he was always going on the attic and writing letters to his in-laws. One of them was the grand rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH] in Johannesburg. Letters are written to his brothers-- a rabbi in Brooklyn, a rabbi in Brooklyn-- written-- written to his family a lot--

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --telling them what is going on with him and what I was done to him. Always, letters are written when I was advancing him with some money. The money, when you are asking me where from, they came, they come from-- from that to shut me up, my mouth-- from the money what I was getting from them, from the Pikoris.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with this extra money that you were receiving, apart from helping these people and these families, did it ease your life? Did it make your life easier in the-- by the fact that you could purchase extra provisions for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. That-- that-- what I was doing, that I brought this out from my home, from my father, to help people, not to live for yourself, but to think about others too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about that rabbi Lvowicz-- Lvowicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lvowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lvowicz-- was writing to family overseas. Does that mean there was a mail service existing in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was no mail list. It was only letters. He had his own-- he was himself. He didn't know what will be, and just telling who was-- what I was doing to him and how he could leave, that he could leave in such bad times, that he could leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- these notes weren't sent anywhere, though. They were kept as a record of the-- of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A record what I was doing, actually-- writing about-- mostly, mostly writing about me, what I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were living in Kailis and working at the tannery, did you receive any news, or you were aware of what was going on in the rest of Europe and Poland in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were getting news from people that they escape. They came, and they tell us what is going on there-- especially to contract what I've got, what I got more with my father, the correspondence what went through Poles. Because Jews, being in ghetto in Radom, couldn't write. It that correct? The same thing applies to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a family in-- in Radom, Bolesta-- Bolesta. Through them, letters were written to me, to a family in Vilna. But I can't recollect the-- the name of the-- the family in Vilna. Could you spell the Bolesta name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-L-E-S-T-A. And because my father bring-- that was-- that wasn't in-- in the time when the war broke out already between-- that was before. I'm talking-- sorry. I'm talking about-- it's mixed up a little bit. I'm talking about the time when I was working for the Russian army, Red Army, and before for [NON-ENGLISH] in the Omega, straightaway at the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got the letters. And my father asked me to help him, to help them, to help them-- the daughter of Dr-- of the director, of Jack, that what I mentioned to you already in the-- previously. So you had no-- are you saying you had no contact--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I haven't got--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: --no contact whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you aid any other people in any other way apart from financially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, I have to mention here things what are not-- it's hard for me to talk about this. But still, I have to tell it. When Vilna start judenrein, become judenrein, and the ghetto was to the concentration, to the extermination camps-- sent away, a day before it happens, that that Rabbi Lvowicz with the wife-- because they didn't have already the children-- they were taken away-- two children-- a little boy, Nomi, and the other one, I forget the name. One of the commandants from our bock took that Rabbi Lvowicz with the wife and send them to the ghetto, for sending him away to the examination camps. The name of that commandant from the police, from the police there, was Krawczynskae.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-A-W-C-Z-Y-N-S-K-A-E-- Lolek. His first name was Lolek. He came from my work, from the tanneries back. I find out what happens. I ask him what happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you want to have your rabbi back here? The test will cost-- will cost you $300. I told him, Lolek, I accept this. Bring me back the rabbi. You will get the $300. Was Lolek with the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the Jewish police. He commanded the-- the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you gave him--

speaker: interviewee
text: They are different. They were different. I don't blame the whole police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you gave him the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because people that they were working with us in the underground-- they were working with us in-- by the partisan. They have been by the partisan-- commandants. But they have been lower persons like him leaving the-- no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --trying a little with-- with-- with-- with-- with Jewish life. Did you give him the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave him the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he bring the rabbi back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi? No. The money was given to him. When I told him that he is getting the $300, he was sure that he will get the $300. He brought back the rabbi. And a 1/2 an hour, the rabbi was back with the wife-- was back.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a break there and change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
